narrative: As a Black male applicant with a middle-income family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.50 and LSAT score of 30, both below the typical averages of 3.21 and 36.70 seen among past students. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 90% chance that you would fail it, leading directly to the rejection of your application. This high predicted risk means the school views you as unlikely to succeed on the bar without retakes, a key benchmark they use to ensure program outcomes. The decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers and strongly signaled to the model a heightened risk of bar failure. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile of 2, again putting you near the bottom of your class early on and reinforcing concerns about consistent academic performance. Your undergraduate GPA also played a supporting role, pushing the prediction further toward failure due to its below-average level compared to successful bar takers. These three academic metrics were the top drivers, each contributing in the direction of higher failure risk rather than offsetting it toward success. While factors like your full-time status align with the vast majority of students, they weren't enough to counterbalance these core weaknesses. In contrast to typical admitted students, who often rank in the middle or top deciles and have stronger GPAs and LSATs, your early and later undergrad performance painted a picture of ongoing challenges that the model linked to bar struggles. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many from similar circumstances have succeeded with targeted improvements—but the model's focus is narrowly on these predictors of bar passage. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying after demonstrating clearer upward trends. Schools value resilience, and addressing these specific areas could shift your profile dramatically for future cycles.
